prompt: On a public, internet-facing app you find all of the following. Which should you remediate FIRST?

options.text: Unauthenticated SQL injection on the login page (full DB read/write)
Missing X-Content-Type-Options header on static pages
A self-XSS that only affects the attacker's own account
Verbose error message exposing a stack trace on a 500 page

explanation: Prioritise by risk = likelihood x impact. Unauthenticated SQLi on an internet-facing endpoint is both easy to exploit and catastrophic (full database compromise), so it goes first. The others are real but lower-risk: a missing header is defence-in-depth, self-XSS harms only the attacker, and a stack trace is information disclosure.
source_reference: OWASP Risk Rating Methodology; FIRST CVSS v3.1

prompt: Two findings on an internet-facing app: (1) IDOR exposing other customers' invoices to any logged-in user; (2) clickjacking on the public marketing homepage. Which is higher priority?

options.text: The IDOR — it directly exposes other users' data and is trivially exploitable
The clickjacking — missing X-Frame-Options is always critical
They are identical in severity because both are web bugs
Neither needs fixing until a pentest confirms them

explanation: The IDOR is a confidentiality breach of real customer data, reachable by any authenticated user — high risk. Clickjacking on a static marketing page with no sensitive actions is low impact. Severity depends on data sensitivity and exploitability, not on the vulnerability's name.
source_reference: OWASP Risk Rating Methodology; CWE-639; CWE-1021

prompt: A finding has CATASTROPHIC impact but is only exploitable by a fully-authenticated admin on an internal-only host. How should you weigh it against an easy-to-exploit, medium-impact bug on the public internet?

options.text: Combine impact AND likelihood — the easy internet-facing bug often warrants faster remediation despite lower impact
Always fix the highest-impact bug first, regardless of how it is exploited
Impact is irrelevant; only exploitability matters
Internal-only bugs never need fixing

explanation: Risk is a function of BOTH impact and likelihood/exploitability. A bug requiring admin access on an internal host has low likelihood, so an easily exploitable internet-facing medium-impact bug can present more real-world risk. (Internal bugs still matter for defence-in-depth and insider/lateral-movement scenarios.)
source_reference: FIRST CVSS v3.1 (Exploitability vs Impact); OWASP Risk Rating Methodology

prompt: Which factor most legitimately LOWERS the practical severity of a vulnerability in production?

options.text: An effective compensating control (e.g. the endpoint is reachable only via VPN with MFA)
The vulnerability having a high CWE number
The code being written in a memory-safe language
The bug being found by a senior engineer

explanation: Compensating controls and reduced exposure lower real-world likelihood, and therefore practical severity — this is the 'Environmental' dimension in CVSS. The CWE number, the language, and who found it do not change how exploitable or impactful the bug is.
source_reference: FIRST CVSS v3.1 (Environmental metrics); OWASP Risk Rating Methodology

prompt: App A's error page leaks a full SQL query and DB schema; App B's leaks a generic message with a random trace id. Which is higher severity and why?

options.text: App A — it reveals information (SQL, schema) that directly helps an attacker plan further attacks
App B — random trace ids are always sensitive
They are equal because both return an error page
App B — returning any error at all is the real problem

explanation: The severity of information exposure (CWE-209) depends on WHAT leaks. App A hands an attacker the database structure and queries (very useful for SQLi/recon); App B's opaque trace id reveals nothing actionable. Same CWE, very different severity — context decides.
